concept_name: Magnetic Field of a Circular Current Loop — B = μ₀NI/2R
source_book: DC Pandey Class 12 Ch.4 Moving Charges and Magnetism §4.6 (Magnetic field on the axis of a circular current loop) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the Indian real-world anchor only. The atomic concept taught here is the FIELD a circular loop produces: a coil of current I, radius R, N turns makes a magnetic field of magnitude B = μ₀NI/2R at its centre, axial (perpendicular to the loop plane, direction by the right-hand grip rule), built because every current element's contribution dB at the centre points the same axial way and they SUPERPOSE (add, never cancel); and on the axis at distance z the field is B(z) = μ₀NIR²/2(R²+z²)^(3/2), maximal at the centre and falling off with z. The deepest student confusions targeted: (M1) the centre field lies in the loop's plane or circles like a wire; (M2) the dB's from opposite sides cancel; (M3) the centre field is the straight-wire formula μ₀I/2πR; (M4) a bigger loop gives a stronger field; (M5) reversing the current does not change B's direction; (M6) the axial field is uniform or strongest off-centre. CUT-LINE: this concept does NOT cover the dipole / bar-magnet identity or the torque on the loop (that is current_loop_acts_as_dipole / torque_on_current_loop_in_field), nor the single-element Biot-Savart law itself (assumed from the prerequisite biot_savart_law), nor the solenoid field B = μ₀nI (magnetic_field_solenoid). Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; misconceptions confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the tangent galvanometer in your school or college physics lab — a vertical circular coil of many turns with a small compass needle sitting right at its centre. Send a current I through the coil and it makes a magnetic field B = μ₀NI/2R straight through the centre, along the coil's axis; the needle swings to follow it. The deflection you read off the scale in your practical exam is exactly this centre field at work — more turns or more current swings the needle harder, a bigger coil swings it less.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the induction cooktop on the kitchen counter at home. Under the glass is a flat coil wound as many tight circular turns. When it switches on, a current runs round those turns and pushes a strong magnetic field up through the centre — straight up into the steel pan. That axial field, the same B = μ₀NI/2R built from many turns adding together, is what heats the pan.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture a single round turn of an electromagnet or a relay coil. The field that grabs the iron core sits along the axis of that loop, strongest right at its centre — the same loop field, just one turn of it. Stack many such turns and you have a solenoid; understand one loop first and the rest follows.

N.name: Number of turns in the loop

z.name: Axial distance from the loop center

dl.name: Length of one representative current element (illustrative, STATE_2)

B_axis.formula: mu_0 * N * I * R * R / (2 * (R*R + z*z) * sqrt(R*R + z*z))

B_axis_uT.formula: (mu_0 * N * I * R * R / (2 * (R*R + z*z) * sqrt(R*R + z*z))) * 1e6

formulas.dB_direction: dB ∥ dl × r̂ — ⊥ to the loop plane (axial), since dl and r̂ both lie in the plane
formulas.grip_rule: Curl the right hand with the current I; the thumb gives the field B along the axis

constraints: At the loop center every element's dB is purely axial: dl and r̂ both lie in the loop plane, so dl × r̂ is ⊥ the plane.
dB from opposite elements ADD at the center (both point the same axial way) — they never cancel.
On the axis with z ≠ 0 each dB has a transverse part, but those cancel by ring symmetry; only the axial component survives.
B_center ∝ N, ∝ I, ∝ 1/R — a bigger loop gives a WEAKER center field.
B(z) is maximal at z = 0 and falls monotonically with |z|; exactly B(z=0) = μ₀NI/2R.
Reversing the current reverses both dB and B (dl flips → dl × r̂ flips).

aha_moment.statement: Every element's dB points the same axial way — so they all add.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: STATE_3 walks round the ring starting from the opposite element; every dB swings to the same axial direction and the stack only grows, never cancels.

scene_composition.text: A circular coil — current I, radius R, N turns

Amber dots = current I
in-plane line = radius R
N = turns

Like the lab tangent
galvanometer — a compass
sits at the centre O.
Question: how strong is the field B right at the centre O?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a circular coil. It carries current I, has radius R, and N turns.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ एक circular coil है। यह current I carry करती है, इसकी radius R है, और N turns हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ ఒక circular coil ఉంది. ఇది current I carry చేస్తుంది, దీనికి radius R ఉంది, మరియు N turns ఉన్నాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the current I march around the ring.
tts_sentences.text_hi: current I को ring के चारों ओर march करते हुए देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: current I ring చుట్టూ march అవ్వడాన్ని గమనించండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: How strong is the magnetic field B right at the center O?
tts_sentences.text_hi: center O पर magnetic field B कितना strong है?
tts_sentences.text_te: center O వద్ద magnetic field B ఎంత strong గా ఉంది?

scene_composition.text: Zoom in: one tiny element dl

Predict before the reveal:
this element makes a field dB
at the centre — which way
does it point?

dB = dl × r̂ — straight OUT
along the axis (⊥ the plane),
not in the plane, not along
the current.
dB = (μ₀/4π)·I dl / R²

tts_sentences.text_en: Zoom in on one tiny current element dl on the ring.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ring पर एक tiny current element dl पर zoom in कीजिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ring పై ఉన్న ఒక tiny current element dl పై zoom in చేయండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Draw the unit vector r-hat from this element straight to the center O.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इस element से center O तक सीधा unit vector r-hat खींचिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఈ element నుండి center O వరకు నేరుగా unit vector r-hat గీయండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: This element makes a field dB at the center — which way does it point?
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह element center पर एक field dB बनाता है — यह किस way में point करता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: ఈ element center వద్ద ఒక field dB ని చేస్తుంది — ఇది ఏ way లో point చేస్తుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: The field dB is dl crossed with r-hat — and it points straight out along the axis.
tts_sentences.text_hi: field dB, dl crossed with r-hat है — और यह axis के साथ सीधा out point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: field dB, dl crossed with r-hat అవుతుంది — మరియు అది axis వెంబడి నేరుగా out point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Its size: the field dB equals mu-naught over four pi, times I d-l over R squared.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसका size: field dB equals mu-naught over four pi, times I d-l over R squared है।
tts_sentences.text_te: దీని size: field dB equals mu-naught over four pi, times I d-l over R squared.

misconception_watch.belief: The field at the center lies in the loop's plane, circles the wire, or points along the current.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The dB arrow pops straight OUT along the axis (⊥ the plane); the hand shows dl and r̂ both lying in the plane.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: dl and r̂ both lie in the loop's plane, so their cross product dl × r̂ — and therefore dB — is axial, perpendicular to the plane.

STATE_3.title: Go round the ring: add, not cancel

scene_composition.text: Walk every element round the ring

The opposite element makes its
own dB too. Going round the
whole ring — do the dB's
CANCEL, or ADD?

Every dB points the SAME way,
straight up the axis. They stack
into one growing axial bundle —
they ADD.
Opposite sides never cancel here — at the centre both point axial.

tts_sentences.text_en: Keep that one field dB. Now go all the way round the ring.
tts_sentences.text_hi: उस एक field dB को रखिए। अब पूरे ring के चारों ओर जाइए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ ఒక field dB ని ఉంచుకోండి. ఇప్పుడు ring చుట్టూ పూర్తిగా వెళ్ళండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Element by element — do the dB's cancel, or do they add?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Element by element — क्या dB's cancel होते हैं, या वे add होते हैं?
tts_sentences.text_te: Element by element — dB's cancel అవుతాయా, లేదా add అవుతాయా?

tts_sentences.text_en: Look at the opposite element first — its field dB also points the same way, straight up the axis.
tts_sentences.text_hi: पहले opposite element को देखिए — उसका field dB भी उसी तरह, सीधा axis पर point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మొదట opposite element ని చూడండి — దాని field dB కూడా అదే విధంగా, నేరుగా axis పైకి point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Every element's field dB points the same axial way — so they all add.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हर element का field dB उसी axial way में point करता है — तो वे सब add होते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ప్రతి element యొక్క field dB అదే axial way లో point చేస్తుంది — కాబట్టి అవన్నీ add అవుతాయి.

misconception_watch.belief: The dB's from opposite sides of the loop cancel at the center.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Walking the ring from the opposite element, every dB swings to the same axial direction; the stack only grows.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: At the center both opposite elements' dB's point the same axial way, so they add — they never cancel.

scene_composition.text: The bundle collapses to one field B

B = μ₀NI / 2R
Clean, axial, largest right
here at the centre.
More turns N → stronger.

Straight wire: μ₀I / 2πR
Loop centre: μ₀NI / 2R
So it's 2R, NOT 2πR —
and a bigger R makes it weaker.
The field B threads through the loop, along its axis.

tts_sentences.text_en: Add every element's field dB and the bundle collapses to one bold arrow — the center field B.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हर element के field dB को add कीजिए और bundle एक bold arrow में collapse हो जाता है — center field B।
tts_sentences.text_te: ప్రతి element యొక్క field dB ని add చేయండి, మరియు bundle ఒక bold arrow లోకి collapse అవుతుంది — center field B.

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnetic field B equals mu-naught N I over 2R. Clean, axial, largest right here at the center.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnetic field B equals mu-naught N I over 2R. Clean, axial, यहीं center में सबसे largest।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnetic field B equals mu-naught N I over 2R. Clean, axial, సరిగ్గా ఇక్కడ center లో largest.

tts_sentences.text_en: Compare the straight wire: its field is mu-naught I over 2-pi-R.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Straight wire की तुलना कीजिए: उसका field है mu-naught I over 2-pi-R।
tts_sentences.text_te: Straight wire తో పోల్చండి: దాని field mu-naught I over 2-pi-R.

tts_sentences.text_en: So the loop gives 2R, not 2-pi-R — and the magnetic field B threads through the loop.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो loop 2R देता है, 2-pi-R नहीं — और magnetic field B loop से thread करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి loop 2R ఇస్తుంది, 2-pi-R కాదు — మరియు magnetic field B loop గుండా threads అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: More turns N raise the magnetic field B in step; a bigger radius R makes it weaker.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ज़्यादा turns N magnetic field B को step में raise करते हैं; एक bigger radius R इसे weaker बनाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఎక్కువ turns N magnetic field B ని step లో raise చేస్తాయి; ఒక bigger radius R దీన్ని weaker చేస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: The center field is the straight-wire formula μ₀I/2πR.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The wire's circular field is drawn alongside the loop's axial field: 2R, not 2πR.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A loop's center field is B = μ₀NI/2R — it is 2R, not 2πR, and it threads through the loop instead of circling a wire.

misconception_watch.belief: A bigger loop gives a stronger center field.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: As R grows, the center field arrow and the readout shrink.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: B ∝ 1/R, so a bigger radius gives a WEAKER center field — double R and B halves.

STATE_5.title: Which way? Grip rule, and the flip

scene_composition.text: Direction by the right-hand grip rule

Curl your fingers WITH the
current I; your thumb points
along the field B, up the axis.

Now reverse the current I —
which way does the field B
point? Predict, then watch.
Current flips → hand re-curls → the field B flips the other way.

tts_sentences.text_en: Which way does the center field B point? Use the right-hand grip rule.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Center field B किस तरफ point करता है? Right-hand grip rule का उपयोग कीजिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: Center field B ఏ వైపు point చేస్తుంది? Right-hand grip rule ఉపయోగించండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Curl your fingers with the current I; your thumb points along the magnetic field B, up the axis.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अपनी fingers को current I के साथ curl कीजिए; आपका thumb magnetic field B के साथ, axis पर ऊपर की ओर point करेगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీ fingers ని current I తో curl చేయండి; మీ thumb magnetic field B తో పాటు, axis పైకి point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now reverse the current I — which way does the magnetic field B point?
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब current I को reverse कीजिए — magnetic field B किस तरफ point करेगा?
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు current I ని reverse చేయండి — magnetic field B ఏ వైపు point చేస్తుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: The current I flips, the hand re-curls, and the field B flips to point the other way.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Current I flip होता है, hand फिर से re-curls करता है, और field B पलट कर दूसरी तरफ point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Current I flip అవుతుంది, hand మళ్ళీ re-curls అవుతుంది, మరియు field B తిరిగి వేరే వైపు point చేస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: Reversing the current does not change the direction of B.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: When the current dots reverse, the grip hand re-curls and the center field arrow flips a full 180°.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Reverse the current and dl flips, so dl × r̂ flips — the field B reverses to point the opposite way along the axis.

STATE_6.title: Off the centre: B(z) falls away

scene_composition.text: Step out along the axis by z

Two distinct lengths here:
R = radius (in the plane)
z = distance along the axis.
Slide out — where is B strongest?

Tallest at the centre (z = 0),
weaker as z grows — the bar and
the B-vs-z curve fall together.
B(z) = μ₀NIR² / 2(R²+z²)^(3/2)

tts_sentences.text_en: Step off the center along the axis by a distance z.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Center से axis के साथ एक distance z दूर हटिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: Center నుండి axis వెంబడి ఒక distance z దూరం జరగండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: As you slide out along the axis, where is the magnetic field B strongest?
tts_sentences.text_hi: जैसे-जैसे आप axis के साथ बाहर slide करते हैं, magnetic field B सबसे strongest कहाँ है?
tts_sentences.text_te: మీరు axis వెంబడి బయటకు slide చేస్తున్నప్పుడు, magnetic field B ఎక్కడ strongest గా ఉంటుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: Slide out and the axial field B of z shrinks — tallest at the center, weaker as z grows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: बाहर slide कीजिए और axial field B of z छोटा होता जाता है — center पर सबसे tallest, और z बढ़ने पर weaker।
tts_sentences.text_te: బయటకు slide చేయండి, axial field B of z తగ్గుతుంది — center వద్ద tallest, z పెరిగే కొద్దీ weaker.

tts_sentences.text_en: The axial field B of z equals mu-naught N I R squared, over 2 times R squared plus z squared, to the three-halves.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Axial field B of z equals mu-naught N I R squared, over 2 times R squared plus z squared, to the three-halves।
tts_sentences.text_te: Axial field B of z equals mu-naught N I R squared, over 2 times R squared plus z squared, to the three-halves.

misconception_watch.belief: The axial field is uniform, or strongest somewhere off the center.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Sliding the sample point out along z, the field bar and the B-vs-z curve fall monotonically; both are tallest at z = 0.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: B(z) is largest at the center (z = 0) and falls monotonically as z grows — never uniform, never peaking off-center.

STATE_7.title: Your turn — I, R, N, z

scene_composition.text: Explore: drag I, R, N, z

Watch the centre field B and the
axial field B(z) update live
in the readout as you drag.

Challenge:
Double N — does B double?
Double R — what happens to B?
Bigger I or more N strengthen B; a bigger R weakens it.

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Drag current I, radius R, turns N, and axial distance z.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आपकी बारी। Current I, radius R, turns N, और axial distance z को drag कीजिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీ వంతు. Current I, radius R, turns N, మరియు axial distance z ని drag చేయండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the center field B and the axial field B of z update live in the readout.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Readout में center field B और axial field B of z को live update होते देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: Readout లో center field B మరియు axial field B of z live గా update అవ్వడం చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Bigger current I or more turns N strengthen the field; a bigger radius R weakens it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Bigger current I या ज़्यादा turns N field को strengthen करते हैं; एक bigger radius R इसे weaken करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Bigger current I లేదా ఎక్కువ turns N field ని strengthen చేస్తాయి; ఒక bigger radius R దీన్ని weaken చేస్తుంది.

STATE_1.label: A coil — current I marching, no field yet
STATE_1.caption: A circular coil: current I, radius R, N turns. How strong is the field B at the center O?

STATE_2.label: One element dl, its r̂, and the axial dB
STATE_2.caption: One element dl. Its field dB at the center points straight out along the axis (dB ∥ dl × r̂).

STATE_3.label: Walk the ring — every dB axial, the stack grows (PRIMARY AHA)
STATE_3.caption: Go round the ring: every element's dB points the same axial way, so they ADD — they never cancel.

STATE_4.label: Bundle merges to one B; B = μ₀NI/2R; wire-vs-loop compare
STATE_4.caption: The bundle collapses to one bold axial B = μ₀NI/2R — 2R, not 2πR; N linear, B ∝ 1/R.

STATE_5.label: Grip rule sets B up the axis; reverse current → B flips
STATE_5.caption: Right-hand grip: fingers curl with I, thumb gives B up the axis. Reverse the current and B flips.

STATE_6.label: Slide along z; B(z) and the B-vs-z curve fall together
STATE_6.caption: Two distinct lengths — radius R in the plane, distance z along the axis. B(z) is tallest at z = 0.

STATE_7.label: Sandbox — I, R, N, z sliders with a live B readout
STATE_7.caption: Drag I, R, N and z. Centre field B = μ₀NI/2R and axial field B(z) update live in the readout.

regeneration_variants.label: Induction cooktop coil
regeneration_variants.physical_world: The flat multi-turn coil under an induction cooktop's glass top
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Many circular turns add to a strong axial field B = μ₀NI/2R pushed up through the centre into the pan
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Flat coil seen from above-and-side, axial field arrow up through the centre; raise N to strengthen B.

regeneration_variants.label: Relay / electromagnet single turn
regeneration_variants.physical_world: One round turn of a relay or electromagnet coil around an iron core
regeneration_variants.same_physics: A single loop's field is axial and strongest at its centre, B = μ₀NI/2R with N = 1
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: One bright loop, axial B arrow through the centre; a faint stack of turns hints at the solenoid to come.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can state that a circular coil of N turns, radius R, carrying current I produces a magnetic field of magnitude B = μ₀NI/2R at its centre; that this field is AXIAL — perpendicular to the loop's plane, with its direction given by the right-hand grip rule (curl the fingers with the current, the thumb gives B) so that reversing the current reverses B; that the centre field is built by SUPERPOSITION — every current element's contribution dB points the same axial way (dB ∥ dl × r̂, with dl and r̂ both in the plane) so the dB's ADD rather than cancel; that the centre formula is 2R not 2πR (it is NOT the straight-wire field μ₀I/2πR) and that, since B ∝ N and B ∝ 1/R, more turns strengthen the field while a bigger loop weakens it (double R halves B); and that along the axis B(z) = μ₀NIR²/2(R²+z²)^(3/2) is maximal at the centre (z = 0) and falls off monotonically with distance. (The dipole / bar-magnet identity, the torque on the loop, and the solenoid field B = μ₀nI are downstream concepts, not assessed by this conceptual V1 quiz.)

questions.stem: A circular coil of N turns and radius R carries a current I. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the CENTRE of the coil?

distractor_misconceptions.B: Uses the straight-wire field μ₀I/2πR for the loop centre and drops the N turns
distractor_misconceptions.C: Keeps the 2π from the straight-wire formula instead of the loop's 2R
distractor_misconceptions.D: Makes the field grow with R instead of falling as 1/R

questions.tested_idea: The field at the centre of an N-turn circular loop is B = μ₀NI/2R.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A flat coil of N circular turns of radius R carries current I. Write the magnetic field at the very centre of the coil.

questions.stem: In which direction does the magnetic field at the centre of a current loop point?

options.A: Along the axis, perpendicular to the loop's plane — direction set by the right-hand grip rule
options.B: In the plane of the loop, pointing along the current
options.C: Radially outward from the centre, within the plane of the loop
options.D: It has no single direction — it circles the wire like a straight wire's field

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks the centre field lies in the loop's plane, along the current
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the centre field points radially in the plane of the loop
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the loop's field circles the wire like a straight wire's field

questions.tested_idea: The centre field is axial (⊥ the loop plane), with direction by the right-hand grip rule.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Looking along the axis of a current-carrying coil, which way does the magnetic field at its centre point, and how do you find that direction?

questions.stem: Going round the ring, take one current element and the element directly opposite it. At the centre, do their field contributions dB add or cancel, and why?

options.A: They add — at the centre every element's dB points the same way, straight along the axis
options.B: They cancel — opposite elements carry current in opposite directions, so their fields subtract
options.C: They partly cancel, leaving a small leftover field in the plane of the loop
options.D: Only the nearer element matters; the opposite one is too far away to count

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks opposite elements' dB's cancel because the current runs the opposite way
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks a leftover in-plane component survives at the centre
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the far element's contribution is negligible at the centre

questions.tested_idea: At the centre every element's dB is axial and points the same way, so opposite contributions ADD, never cancel.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Pick two diametrically opposite bits of a current loop. At the centre of the loop, how do their magnetic-field contributions combine, and why?

questions.stem: By the Biot-Savart law, in which direction does the field dB of a SINGLE current element point at the centre of the loop?

options.A: Straight out along the axis (⊥ the loop plane), because dB ∥ dl × r̂ and both dl and r̂ lie in the plane
options.B: Along the current element dl, tangent to the ring
options.C: From the element straight toward the centre, along r̂ (radially)
options.D: Around the element in tiny circles, like a straight wire's field

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks dB points along the current element dl
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks dB points radially, along r̂ toward the centre
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks dB circles the element like a straight wire's field

questions.tested_idea: One element's dB at the centre is axial because dB ∥ dl × r̂ and both dl and r̂ lie in the plane.
questions.parallel_form_stem: For one short element of a circular loop, use dB ∥ dl × r̂ to say which way that element's field points at the loop's centre.

questions.stem: Along the axis of a current loop, where is the magnetic field strongest?

options.A: Exactly at the centre (z = 0); it falls off monotonically as you move out along the axis
options.B: At some distance off the centre, then it drops away on either side
options.C: It is the same everywhere along the axis — the axial field is uniform
options.D: Far out along the axis, where the whole loop comes into view

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks the axial field peaks off-centre rather than at z = 0
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the axial field is uniform along the axis
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the field grows stronger far out along the axis

questions.tested_idea: B(z) is maximal at z = 0 and falls monotonically with distance along the axis.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Moving a probe along the axis of a current loop, at what point does it read the largest magnetic field, and how does the reading change as it moves away?

questions.stem: You double the radius R of the loop, keeping the current I and the number of turns N the same. The magnetic field at the centre:

options.A: Halves, because B = μ₀NI/2R is inversely proportional to R
options.B: Doubles, because a bigger loop makes a stronger field
options.C: Stays the same, because R does not affect the centre field
options.D: Quadruples, because the loop's area grows as R²

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks a bigger loop gives a stronger centre field
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the radius does not affect the centre field
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the field scales with the area R² rather than 1/R

questions.tested_idea: Since B = μ₀NI/2R ∝ 1/R, doubling R halves the centre field.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Keeping I and N fixed, you make the coil twice as wide (double R). By what factor does the centre field change?
